title: Castle in the Air - Chapter Three: In which Flower-in-the-Night discovers several important facts (Fragment 1)
text:
Castle in the Air

Chapter Three

I N WHICH F LOWER-IN-THE -N IGHT DISCOVERS SEVERAL IMPORTANT FACTS

Abdullah carefully tied the carpet around the roof pole again and went out into the Bazaar, where he sought out the booth of the most skillful of the various artists who traded there.

After the usual opening courtesies, in which Abdullah called the artist prince of the pencil and enchanter with chalks and the artist retorted by calling Abdullah cream of customers and duke of discernment, Abdullah said, “I want drawings of every size, shape, and kind of man that you have ever seen. Draw me kings and paupers, merchants and workmen, fat and thin, young and old, handsome and ugly, and also plain average. If some of these are kinds of men that you have never seen, I require you to invent them, O paragon of the paintbrush. And if your invention fails, which I hardly think is likely, O aristocrat of artists, then all you need do is turn your eyes outward, gaze, and copy!”

Abdullah flung out one arm to point to the teeming, rushing crowds shopping in the Bazaar. He was moved almost to tears at the thought that this everyday sight was something Flower-in-the-Night had never seen.

The artist drew his hand dubiously down his straggly beard. “For sure, noble admirer of mankind,” he said, “this I can do easily. But could the jewel of judgment perhaps inform this humble draftsman what these many portraits of men are needed for?”

“Why should the crown and diadem of the drawing board wish to know this?” Abdullah asked, rather dismayed.

“Assuredly, the chieftain of customers will understand that this crooked worm needs to know what medium to use,” the artist replied. In fact, he was simply curious about this most unusual order. “Whether I paint in oils on wood or canvas, in pen upon paper or vellum, or even in fresco upon a wall depends on what this pearl among patrons wishes to do with the portraits.”

“Ah, paper, please,” Abdullah said hastily. He had no wish to make his meeting with Flower-in-the-Night public. It was clear to him that her father must be a very rich man who would certainly object to a young carpet merchant’s showing her other men besides this Prince of Ochinstan. “The portraits are for an invalid who has never been able to walk abroad as other men do.”

“Then you are a champion of charity,” said the artist, and he agreed to draw the pictures for a surprisingly small sum. “No, no, child of fortune, do not thank me,” he said when Abdullah tried to express his gratitude. “My reasons are three. First, I have laid by me many portraits which I do for my own pleasure, and to charge you for those is not honest since I would have drawn them anyway. Second, the task you set is ten times more interesting than my usual work, which is to do portraits of young women or their bridegrooms, or of horses and camels, all of whom I have to make handsome, regardless of reality; or else to paint rows of sticky children whose parents wish them to seem like angels—again regardless of reality. And my third reason is that I think you are mad, my most noble of customers, and to exploit you would be unlucky.”

It became known almost immediately, all over the Bazaar, that young Abdullah, the carpet merchant, had lost his reason and would buy any portraits that people had for sale.

This was a great nuisance to Abdullah. For the rest of that day he was constantly being interrupted by persons arriving with long and flowery speeches about this portrait of their grandmother which only poverty would induce them to part with; or this portrait of the Sultan’s racing camel which happened to fall off the back of a cart; or this locket containing a picture of their sister. It took Abdullah much time to get rid of these people—and on several occasions he did actually buy a painting or drawing if the subject was a man. That, of course, kept people coming.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Three: In which Flower-in-the-Night discovers several important facts

vocabulary.definition: A person who has a feeling of respect or approval for someone or something.
vocabulary.example: For sure, noble admirer of mankind, this I can do easily.

vocabulary.definition: A member of the highest social class, often associated with nobility; here used metaphorically to mean a master of an art.
vocabulary.example: O aristocrat of artists, then all you need do is turn your eyes outward.

vocabulary.definition: The voluntary giving of help, typically money, to those in need.
vocabulary.example: Then you are a champion of charity, said the artist.

vocabulary.definition: Polite acts or remarks that show respect or consideration for others.
vocabulary.example: After the usual opening courtesies, Abdullah called the artist prince of the pencil.

ru.translation: фамильярное слово для женщины, выращивающей урожай (не встречается в тексте)

vocabulary.definition: Not applicable (word not in text).
The ability to judge well; perceptiveness.
vocabulary.example: Abdullah said, 'cream of customers and duke of discernment'.

vocabulary.definition: To regard or treat with haughty contempt; to look down upon.
vocabulary.example: Not in text, but similar advanced verbs were sought.

vocabulary.definition: Distress caused by something unexpected; disappointment.
vocabulary.example: Abdullah asked, rather dismayed, why to know the portraits purpose.

vocabulary.definition: Hesitating or doubting; not to be relied upon or suspect.
vocabulary.example: The artist drew his hand dubiously down his straggly beard.

vocabulary.definition: A person who uses magic or casts spells; metaphorically, someone very skilled in creating a magical or delightful effect.
vocabulary.example: Abdullah called the artist prince of the pencil and enchanter with chalks.

vocabulary.definition: To make full use of and benefit from (a resource or person), often unfairly.
vocabulary.example: I think you are mad and to exploit you would be unlucky.

vocabulary.definition: A technique of mural painting executed upon freshly laid lime plaster.
vocabulary.example: Whether I paint in oils or even in fresco upon a wall.

vocabulary.definition: A person made weak or disabled by illness or injury.
vocabulary.example: The portraits are for an invalid who has never been able to walk abroad.

vocabulary.definition: To create or design something that has not existed before.
vocabulary.example: If some of these are kinds of men that you have never seen, I require you to invent them.

vocabulary.definition: A small ornamental case, typically on a chain, worn around the neck and used for keeping a photograph or memento.
vocabulary.example: This locket containing a picture of their sister.

vocabulary.definition: A means or material used by an artist to create their work.
vocabulary.example: This crooked worm needs to know what medium to use.

vocabulary.definition: A person, thing, or circumstance causing inconvenience or annoyance.
vocabulary.example: This was a great nuisance to Abdullah, as people kept coming.

vocabulary.definition: A person or thing regarded as a perfect example of a particular quality.
vocabulary.example: I require you to invent them, O paragon of the paintbrush.

vocabulary.definition: To reply quickly and sharply, often in a witty or angry manner.
vocabulary.example: The artist retorted by calling Abdullah cream of customers.

vocabulary.definition: Growing or spreading irregularly or unevenly.
vocabulary.example: He drew his hand dubiously down his straggly beard.

vocabulary.definition: Abundantly filled, overflowing with things or people.
vocabulary.example: He was moved at the thought that this everyday sight was something Flower-in-the-Night had never seen — the teeming, rushing crowds.

vocabulary.definition: A fine parchment made originally from the skin of a calf, used for writing or painting.
vocabulary.example: Whether I paint in oils on wood or canvas, in pen upon paper or vellum.
